title: Stratospheric ozone concentration projections
subtitle: Stratospheric ozone concentrations with projections to 2100 based on chemistry-climate models. These are measured as global and regional averages relative to levels in 1960 (1960 = 0).
caption:
Stratospheric ozone concentration projections

Stratospheric ozone concentrations with projections to 2100 based on chemistry-climate models. These are measured as global and regional averages relative to levels in 1960 (1960 = 0).

This line chart displays stratospheric ozone concentration projections from 1960 to 2100, measured as global and regional averages relative to 1960 levels where 1960 equals zero. Three distinct trend lines represent the Arctic, Global, and Antarctic regions. The Arctic line, shown in orange, begins near zero in 1960, rises slightly to approximately 5 by 1970, then declines to a low point around -25 near 2000 before rising steadily to reach approximately 40 by 2100. The Global line, in brown, follows a similar but less extreme pattern, dipping to roughly -15 around 2000 and recovering to just above zero by 2100. The Antarctic line, in blue, shows the most dramatic variation, starting near zero in 1960, falling steeply to approximately -120 around 2005, then rising consistently to reach about -5 by 2100. All three lines converge near zero at the 1960 starting point. The Arctic and Global lines cross the zero baseline around 2030 and 2050 respectively, while the Antarctic line remains below zero throughout the entire period shown. The vertical axis ranges from -140 to 40, with grid lines at intervals of 20. The horizontal axis spans from 1960 to 2100 with tick marks every 20 years. These figures represent the mean across a number of model runs, and model projections have notable uncertainty around such average trends.
